title: Magnesium-graphene interphase boundaries created by high-pressure torsion enhance hydrogen storage kinetics:Mechanisms and significance of activation energy and frequency factor
authors: Runchen Zhou, Payam Edalati, Anthony Alhayek, Shivam Dangwal, Marc Novelli, Md. Amirul Islam, Baran Bidyut Saha, Thierry Grosdidier, Kaveh Edalati
abstract: A strategy to overcome sluggish hydrogenation/dehydrogenation of magnesium is demonstrated by creating magnesium-graphene interphase boundaries via high-pressure torsion (HPT). HPT reduces the grain size of pure magnesium from 1 mm to 850 nm, with 70% of grain boundaries having high misorientation angles. Graphene addition leads to even finer grain sizes of 10-500 nm with a bimodal morphology. The magnesium-graphene composites exhibit superior kinetics at 623 K while maintaining high air resistance. Kinetic modeling reveals that the rate-controlling mechanism transits from interfacial reaction in coarse-grained magnesium to atomic diffusion in magnesium-graphene nanocomposites. Kissinger analysis shows that the activation energy for hydrogen desorption remains unchanged at 145 +/- 2 kJ/mol, regardless of the presence of grain or interphase boundaries. However, the frequency factor (number of successful attempts to overcome the activation energy) increases with the generation of interfaces, which serve as sites for hydrogen diffusion and heterogeneous metal/hydride nucleation. These findings highlight the impact of interphase boundary engineering via severe plastic deformation for enhancing the kinetics and air resistance of hydrogen storage materials.
summary: This paper demonstrates a method to speed up hydrogen storage in magnesium by using high-pressure torsion to create magnesium-graphene interfaces. These interfaces act as high-speed pathways for hydrogen diffusion, significantly increasing the reaction frequency without changing the energy barrier.
main_problem: The slow hydrogen absorption and desorption kinetics of magnesium, which is a promising but kinetically limited hydrogen storage material.
main_result: Creating magnesium-graphene interphase boundaries via high-pressure torsion enhances hydrogen storage kinetics by significantly increasing the frequency factor, without altering the activation energy.
method: High-pressure torsion (HPT) is used for severe plastic deformation and grain refinement, followed by Sieverts-type measurements for kinetics and Kissinger analysis for thermal parameters.
model_or_system: Magnesium (Mg) and magnesium-graphene composites (0, 2, 5, and 10 wt% graphene) processed via high-pressure torsion to create nanostructured bimodal morphologies.
key_observables: Hydrogen absorption/desorption rates, hydrogen storage capacity (wt%), activation energy (Ea), frequency factor (A), and grain size.
important_parameters: Temperature (623 K), hydrogen pressure (4.03 MPa absorption, 0.01 MPa desorption), and graphene weight percentage.
main_assumptions: The study assumes that the kinetic improvement is driven by the density of active sites rather than changes in the fundamental energy barrier.
figures_summary: XRD profiles for phase identification; SEM/EBSD/TEM images showing grain refinement and graphene distribution; DSC/TGA data for thermal analysis; and Kissinger plots showing changes in the frequency factor.
paper_structure: Introduction of the kinetic bottleneck; experimental synthesis via HPT; microstructural characterization (XRD, SEM, TEM); hydrogen storage kinetic measurements; kinetic modeling and Kissinger analysis; and discussion of the mechanism shift from 3D reaction to 2D diffusion.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Mixing of magnesium with graphene by HPT with no chemical reactions. XRD profiles of magnesium mixed with 0, 2, 5 and 10 wt% of graphene by HPT as well as annealed pure magnesium.
Fig. 2. Formation of large fractions of high-angle grain boundaries by HPT. (a) OM image of annealed magnesium and (b) EBSD orientation map of HPT-processed magnesium.

Fig. 3. Distribution of graphene in magnesium by HPT. (a, c, e) SEM images taken from disc center at cross-sectional view and (b, d, f) EDS maps of carbon taken from middle of discs at top view for magnesium mixed with (a, b) 2, (c, d) 5 and (e, f) 10 wt% of graphene by HPT

Fig. 4. Grain refinement by application of HPT to magnesium-graphene composites. TEM (a, d, g, j) bright-field micrographs, (b, e, h, k) SAED analyses and (c, f, i, l) dark-field micrographs for (a- c) annealed pure magnesium, (d-f) HPT-processed pure magnesium and (g-l) magnesium mixed with 5 wt% of graphene by HPT. Dark-field micrographs were generated utilizing diffracted beams indicated by arrows in SAED.

Fig. 5. Formation of dislocation and magnesium-graphene interphase boundaries by HPT. TEM high-resolution images and relevant FFT analyses for (a-c) HPT-processed pure magnesium and (d, e) magnesium mixed with 5 wt% of graphene by HPT. (b) and (c) are magnified images of dislocations shown in (a) and (e) is a magnified view of interphase shown in (d).
